steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Text 2's author confirms that ‘many other predation relief studies show an increase in prey abundance,’ accepting the principle as evidentially supported. But their own study found no such effect on deer — and they explain the discrepancy by noting the principle applies to small, rapidly reproducing prey, not necessarily to large, slowly reproducing prey like deer.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — It is plausible, but many of the studies that support it have methodological flaws.: Text 2 never criticizes the methodology of supporting studies; it accepts their findings. ✗
  • Choice B — It has been challenged by some studies, but the findings of those studies have not been widely accepted.: Text 2 does not discuss the acceptance status of Gulsby's or any other challenging findings. ✗
  • Choice C — It may be true for some predators but only because those predators share certain physical characteristics.: Text 2 frames the limitation around prey characteristics (body size, reproductive rate), not predator characteristics. ✗
  • Choice D — It has some evidential support, but it should not be regarded as universally applicable.: This captures exactly what Text 2 argues — the principle holds in many cases but fails for large, slowly reproducing prey like deer. ✓
That matches Choice D — It has some evidential support, but it should not be regarded as universally applicable..
